title: Diversity-Aware Selection
concept: Avoiding a context window filled with five near-identical high-scoring chunks that add little new information.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, greedy selection under a budget worked by...

options.text: Filling the token budget with the highest-value chunks first, like packing a knapsack
Randomly shuffling chunks until the budget was full
Selecting only the single most relevant chunk and ignoring the rest of the budget

exercises.explain: Greedy selection under a budget works like packing a knapsack: you sort chunks by value (relevance) and keep adding the best ones until you run out of token budget. Today we'll see a wrinkle that pure greedy selection misses.

cards.title: Five Mirrors, One Reflection
cards.body: You give the AI a 5-chunk context window. But all 5 chunks say almost the exact same sentence in slightly different words. The AI just read one idea five times — and missed everything else it could have known.

cards.title: What Is Diversity-Aware Selection?
cards.body: It's a selection strategy that picks chunks which are both relevant AND different from each other, instead of just picking the single highest-scoring chunks over and over. The goal: cover more ground, not repeat the same point five times.

cards.title: Five Copies of the Same Answer
cards.body: Imagine asking five friends for restaurant advice, but they all just repeat the exact same recommendation. You didn't get five opinions — you got one opinion, five times. Diversity-aware selection makes sure your 'friends' (chunks) actually say different things.

cards.title: Research Question Example
cards.body: A user asks 'What causes climate change?' The top 5 chunks by score are all near-identical paragraphs about CO2 emissions from one article. A diversity-aware selector would swap 2-3 of those for chunks about methane, deforestation, or ocean warming — still relevant, but adding new information instead of repeating.

cards.title: Myth: Highest Score Always Wins
cards.body: Myth: 'Just take the top-N highest-scoring chunks and you're done.' Truth: if the top-N are near-duplicates (common when a document repeats itself or many docs cover the same fact), you waste your budget. Smart systems check similarity between chunks, not just similarity to the query.

cards.title: Score-Only vs Diversity-Aware
cards.body: Score-only selection picks whatever ranks highest, even if they're clones of each other. Diversity-aware selection actively checks 'does this chunk add something NEW?' before including it.

left.text: 5 chunks, all near-identical paragraphs about the same single fact — lots of tokens, little new info

right.text: 5 chunks covering different angles: the core fact plus related causes, effects, and context

exercises.prompt: Why is a context window of 5 near-identical high-scoring chunks a problem?

options.text: It uses up budget without adding much new information for the LLM
It makes the LLM's response too short
It causes the embedding model to crash
It always violates the token budget limit

exercises.explain: Near-identical chunks each cost tokens but barely add new content beyond what the first chunk already said. You're spending your limited context budget on repetition instead of coverage.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Diversity-aware selection means you should always avoid picking the highest-scoring chunks.
exercises.explain: False. Diversity-aware selection still values relevance — it just avoids picking multiple chunks that are all saying nearly the same thing. High-scoring chunks are still picked, but redundant ones among them get swapped for chunks that add new angles.

exercises.sentence: If the top 5 chunks are all near-duplicates, diversity-aware selection swaps some out for chunks that add new information instead of just being relevant.
exercises.explain: The core problem diversity-aware selection solves is near-duplicate chunks — text that says the same thing in different words — crowding out useful, different information.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three retrieval strategies is most likely AI-generated marketing copy (vague, buzzword-heavy, lacking real technical substance) rather than a genuine technical explanation?

options.text: Our revolutionary AI-powered synergy engine intelligently curates hyper-relevant insights to unlock unprecedented contextual excellence for your enterprise.
We compute pairwise similarity between candidate chunks and penalize ones too similar to already-selected chunks, favoring variety.
We cap how many chunks can come from the same source document to avoid redundant duplicates in the context window.

exercises.explain: Option A is full of vague buzzwords ('synergy engine', 'unprecedented contextual excellence') with zero concrete mechanism explained — a hallmark of AI-generated filler copy. Options B and C describe specific, implementable techniques (similarity penalties, per-source caps), which is how real engineers talk.

exercises.prompt: A user asks 'How do I reset my password?' Your retriever returns 10 chunks. The top 5 are all near-duplicate paragraphs from the same FAQ page scoring 0.95-0.97 relevance. Chunks 6-8 (score 0.80-0.85) cover related but distinct topics: two-factor auth setup, account lockout, and security questions. What's the smartest context window to build with a 5-chunk budget?

options.text: All 5 of the top-scoring near-duplicate chunks, since they scored highest
A mix: 2-3 of the top near-duplicates plus 2-3 of the diverse related chunks
Only chunk 1, since it alone should be enough
The 5 lowest-scoring chunks, to guarantee variety

exercises.explain: Greedy-by-score-alone would grab all 5 duplicates and waste your whole budget saying the same thing five times. But diversity-aware selection isn't 'ignore relevance' either — you still want the truly relevant top chunks. The smart move balances both: keep a couple of the strong duplicates (they ARE the core answer) but swap remaining slots for chunks that cover different, still-relevant angles (2FA, lockouts, security questions). That way your context window is both relevant AND informative, giving the LLM more useful ground to answer follow-up questions or edge cases. This wraps up Relevance-Based Prioritization — next you'll build on both budget-aware and diversity-aware selection together in real pipelines.
